reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest voice prioritizes: (1) workload feasibility without additional unfunded mandates; (2) professional autonomy in classroom selection and differentiation; (3) staffing stability and adequate support infrastructure. S1's admission-rule redesign, if implemented without corresponding classroom-support resources, imposes workload (managing more heterogeneous intake cohorts) without autonomy (quotas are externally set) or staffing relief.
reactions.interest: Hungarian teacher unions and general-school educators managing admission cohorts and classroom heterogeneity
reactions.public_good_frame: Professional conditions that enable equitable pedagogy: teachers can only reduce SES-outcome gradients if they have time, training, and collegial support to differentiate instruction for more mixed-ability cohorts. Without these, admission reform creates a 'selection illusion' — moving the sorting boundary but not addressing the root instructional inequality.
reactions.argument: S1 is conditionally supportable if and only if it is accompanied by: (a) dedicated, ring-fenced funding for classroom support staff (teaching assistants, co-teachers in heterogeneous streams) in schools receiving higher proportions of low-SES admitted students; (b) mandatory professional development on differentiated instruction, compensated as part of contracted workload, not added as unpaid expectation; (c) transparent, binding class-size caps (max 20 in mixed-ability cohorts, lower than current permitted maximums) to make differentiation materially feasible; (d) explicit workload protections (e.g., reduced non-contact duty for teachers in highest-heterogeneity streams). Without these, S1 risks reproducing current inequities through classroom-level sorting (streaming, ability grouping, informal tracking within mixed-cohort classes), which falls on teachers to manage without resources. The scenario's silence on implementation support for schools receiving reformed admissions is a critical gap from a workload-feasibility perspective.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support S1 if: costed, ring-fenced funding for classroom support staff is legislated alongside admission reform; mandatory professional development on differentiated instruction is paid as part of contracted time (not volunteer); binding class-size reductions for mixed-ability cohorts are written into law; workload protections (reduced non-contact duty, explicit time allocation for differentiation planning) are negotiated with unions. Would oppose S1 if: admission rules are changed without corresponding investment in teacher support, or if support is framed as a moral/professional obligation rather than a funded, protected entitlement.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest voice prioritizes workload feasibility, professional autonomy, and staffing stability. S2's decade-long phase-down creates compounded workload and autonomy risks: (1) Parallel regimes (grandfathered cohorts under old rules + new cohorts under caps) require simultaneous management of two admission and curriculum streams, increasing administrative burden on schools and teachers without additional staffing; (2) the scenario's own assessment acknowledges near-certain non-state offset, meaning state-sector caps do not reduce early selection overall, only shift it, leaving state-sector teachers managing capped, residually more disadvantaged cohorts for a decade with no structural relief; (3) staff reallocation as state selective-track positions shrink threatens job security for teachers in those tracks and requires unpaid retraining/reassignment for others.
reactions.interest: Hungarian teachers in state-maintained schools, especially those currently teaching selective tracks (gimnazium streams)
reactions.public_good_frame: Stable, adequately-resourced teaching conditions enable equitable education; decade-long institutional uncertainty and workload doubling (managing parallel regimes) degrade both. If non-state offset is near-certain, the public-good rationale for state-sector sacrifice is undermined — the reform shifts rather than solves early selection, and state teachers bear the cost.
reactions.argument: S2 imposes sustained, compounding workload without corresponding professional gain or resource allocation. A 10-year grandfathering period means schools and teachers manage two simultaneous admission regimes, two curriculum tracks, and two cohort compositions until the last grandfathered cohort leaves. This is a decade of administrative overhead with no offsetting reduction in early selection (since non-state maintainers absorb displaced demand per the scenario's own risk assessment). State-sector teachers in selective tracks face job insecurity and forced reassignment; general-track teachers face larger, more disadvantaged cohorts without additional support. The scenario's cost analysis labels parallel-regime administration 'medium-to-high' but underestimates teacher workload impact: no reductions in pupil-teacher ratios or non-contact time are proposed to offset the increased cohort complexity. Additionally, the political-risk section identifies this as having 'moderate' precedent for being reversed — meaning teachers invest in retraining and workload adaptation that may be undone by a subsequent government, with no protection of their prior effort.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would conditionally support S2 if: (a) the phase-down is accelerated to 3-5 years (not 10), reducing the parallel-regime administrative burden; (b) legislation explicitly mandates pupil-teacher ratio reductions (max 18 in general-track upper grades) as caps tighten, protecting workload in the cohorts most affected by phase-down; (c) a binding cross-party pact commits to the phase-down regardless of government change, protecting teachers' retraining investment; (d) state-maintained selective-track teachers are offered voluntary transition packages (early retirement, guaranteed reassignment with training support, career-grade protection) rather than forced reallocation. Would oppose S2 if: parallel regimes persist beyond 5 years, workload protections are not legislated, or the reform is vulnerable to political reversal without teacher-protection guarantees.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest voice prioritizes: (1) workload feasibility in managing increased classroom heterogeneity; (2) professional autonomy and adequate training to exercise it; (3) staffing capacity and job security. S3's abolition of early selection is pedagogically coherent with equity goals, BUT it creates severe workload and autonomy risks if implemented without complementary investments in teacher training, classroom support, and reduced class sizes. The scenario's own description flags teacher training as a 'high' cost with 'no precise per-cohort unit cost available' — this is a workload-feasibility red flag, not a neutral gap.
reactions.interest: Hungarian teachers in comprehensive schools managing age 10-14 mixed-ability cohorts; teachers in general schools currently educating non-selected cohorts
reactions.public_good_frame: Comprehensive education can reduce SES-outcome gradients only if teachers have the training, time, and support infrastructure to differentiate instruction effectively for mixed-ability peers. Without these, comprehensiveness becomes a warehousing model — mixing cohorts without enabling equitable pedagogy, increasing workload while reducing autonomy and outcomes.
reactions.argument: S3 is conditionally supportable, but the scenario presents it as coherent without addressing a critical workload and autonomy gap. The mechanism depends on teachers managing sharply increased heterogeneity (10-year-olds spanning from gifted-track capability to significant learning delays in the same classroom). The scenario proposes 'nationwide differentiated-instruction retraining' but flags it as 'high cost' with 'no precise per-cohort unit cost' — meaning the extent of retraining is uncosted and unspecified. From a workload perspective, this is unacceptable: teachers cannot be held accountable for heterogeneous outcomes without explicit, funded, time-allocated training. The scenario's own equity-impact section identifies middle-class flight risk, potentially reconcentrating disadvantage in public comprehensive schools and making the workload burden heaviest where support infrastructure is weakest. Additionally, the scenario flags teacher shortages as a documented constraint but does not propose staffing increases to offset the additional support burden comprehensiveness creates. Professional autonomy is also at risk: the scenario mentions 'compliance audits rather than guidance-based standards,' which unions may view as erosion of pedagogical discretion. Supportable if: (a) differentiated-instruction retraining is legally mandated as paid professional development (minimum 200 hours per teacher, spread over years 1-4), not voluntary; (b) classroom support staff (teaching assistants, remedial specialists) are legislated as compulsory additions to comprehensive-school staffing, with explicit pupil-teacher ratios (max 18 in mixed-ability streams, lower if school serves 30%+ disadvantaged pupils); (c) a binding teacher supply commitment (recruitment, retention bonuses, working-condition improvements) is legislated before abolition, not after; (d) choice-architecture safeguards and maintainer-transparency rules are legally enforceable, not advisory, to minimize flight risk and prevent sorting migration from recreating single-track comprehensiveness in practice.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support S3 if: differentiated-instruction retraining is explicitly costed, legislated as paid time, and delivered before abolition takes effect; classroom support staffing and pupil-teacher ratio reductions are written into law before rollout; a binding teacher-recruitment and retention package is legislated; choice-architecture and maintainer-transparency safeguards are legally enforceable. Would oppose S3 if: retraining is framed as a moral obligation rather than funded entitlement; classroom support or staffing reductions are offered as 'expected resources' without legal guarantee; compliance audits replace guidance-based evaluation; or if the reform is not cross-party protected against political reversal (teachers' retraining investment would be wasted if a successor government reverses the structural change).

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Teacher-interest voice prioritizes workload feasibility, professional autonomy, and equitable working conditions across schools. S4 explicitly leaves the sorting mechanism intact while proposing 'compensation' for residual disadvantage. From a teacher perspective, this is a workload-shifting strategy, not an equitable solution: it concentrates disadvantaged cohorts in general schools (via admission sorting) while proposing to 'offset' this through additional remedial, diagnostic, and retention-reduction work — all falling on general-school teachers without structural changes to class size, peer composition, or resource distribution. The scenario's own equity-impact section describes S4 as 'narrows the SES gradient at best partially' and 'leaves the sorting mechanism itself intact,' acknowledging that it legitimizes rather than solves early selection. For teachers, this means permanent workload asymmetry: selective-track teachers enjoy smaller classes and more homogeneous (higher-performing, higher-SES) cohorts, while general-track teachers manage larger classes, more learning delays, and more remedial/diagnostic work — indefinitely.
reactions.interest: Hungarian general-school teachers managing non-selected cohorts, and by extension, all teachers concerned with professional equity and sustainable workload
reactions.public_good_frame: Equitable public education requires equitable teaching conditions: teachers in disadvantaged schools should not bear disproportionate workload and emotional labor burdens indefinitely as 'compensation' for sorting they did not choose and cannot control. Professional equity is a prerequisite for educational equity — teachers teaching the same curriculum to harder-to-teach cohorts, without structural peer-composition or class-size relief, are being asked to be martyrs, not professionals.
reactions.argument: S4 is fundamentally a burden-shifting policy masquerading as compensation. It leaves early selection intact (the problem) while proposing additional work (grade-retention reduction, diagnostic support, remedial curriculum) for the teachers most affected by sorting (general-track teachers managing the cohorts not selected into selective tracks). This is not equity; it is structural injustice dressed in compensatory language. The scenario explicitly states S4 'leaves the sorting mechanism itself intact and only compensates downstream' — meaning the root cause of inequality (peer-composition stratification) is untouched, and teachers are asked to compensate through increased workload. General-school teachers will manage cohorts with more learning delays, lower baseline peer performance, and (per the scenario's equity-impact section) lower funding levels despite higher pedagogical complexity — contradicting the stated intent of 'targeted funding.' The scenario also flags budget vulnerability: TEIP-style funding is 'historically the first line item cut during fiscal consolidation,' meaning general-school teachers invest in developing remedial, diagnostic, and retention-reduction expertise, then see funding evaporate, leaving them with the workload but not the resources. This is a formula for burnout, not sustainability. Additionally, S4 legitimizes early selection as a permanent institutional feature ('compensation may entrench rather than dismantle the sorting engine'), meaning selective-track teachers enjoy stable, resourced, low-workload positions indefinitely, while general-track teachers endure high-complexity, under-resourced conditions permanently. This is professionally inequitable and, from a teacher-union perspective, a race-to-the-bottom strategy: it abandons structural reform to maintain status quo for selective-track teachers while asking general-track teachers to absorb indefinite additional burden.
reactions.condition_to_change: Would support S4 only if reframed as a temporary transition package to structural reform (S1 or S3), not a permanent substitute. Specifically: (a) S4's targeted funding, curriculum standards, and reduced grade retention are legislated as time-limited support layers (years 1-5) during a parallel, legally-mandated transition to either admission-rule reform (S1) or delayed tracking (S3); (b) TEIP-style funding is legislated as ring-fenced, multi-parliamentary-term entitlement (minimum 10 years), not vulnerable to consolidation cuts; (c) class-size caps are written into law (max 20 in general-track upper grades, lower if school serves 40%+ learning-support pupils), and selective-track classes are capped at no higher than general-track maximum to eliminate professional workload asymmetry; (d) compensation for general-school teachers (workload allowances, retention bonuses, reduced non-contact duty) are explicitly offered alongside increased resourcing. Would oppose S4 if: it is framed as a permanent substitute for structural reform; if TEIP-style funding is not ring-fenced against budget cuts; if class-size asymmetry between selective and general tracks persists; or if teachers in disadvantaged schools are expected to absorb indefinite additional workload without corresponding professional compensation or structural peer-composition relief.
